programs.name: NJ FamilyCare Aged, Blind, Disabled (ABD) Medicaid Programs
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+ or disabled in New Jersey, this Medicaid program covers their medical costs and may pay their Medicare premiums.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+, blind, or disabled and living on a fixed income in New Jersey, NJ FamilyCare ABD covers nearly all their healthcare costs — including doctor visits, hospital stays, prescriptions, and Medicare premiums if they have Medicare. Because this program has multiple tiers with different income limits, your parent may qualify even if their income seems too high for traditional Medicaid.

For parents who need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, the program also covers long-term care services through managed care plans. If your parent receives SSI, they automatically qualify — no separate application needed.

The program considers your parent's income and assets, with limits ranging from $1,350/month for individuals in the basic tier up to slightly higher amounts in other tiers. Asset limits are $4,000 for single people and $6,000 for couples in most programs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services: must meet nursing facility level of care (clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: No intent to leave New Jersey

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at njfamilycare.org or call your county Board of Social Services — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Gather 5 years of financial records
steps.description: If applying for long-term care services, you'll need 5 years of bank statements, asset transfers, and financial documentation due to the look-back period.

steps.title: Complete the ABD application
steps.description: Use the NJ FamilyCare Aged, Blind, Disabled Program Application online at njfamilycare.org or download the PDF from the state website.

steps.title: Contact your county office
steps.description: Call your county Board of Social Services to submit documents and schedule any required interviews.

steps.title: Get clinical evaluation if needed
steps.description: If your parent needs long-term care services, contact your local ADRC (Area Agency on Aging) for the nursing facility level of care assessment.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent already receives SSI, they're automatically eligible — contact your county office to confirm enrollment rather than starting a new application.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size (2025)
contentSections.footnote: 2025 limits for New Jersey Care/Special Medicaid and NJRAER ABD programs

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Information not detailed in official sources — contact county office for complete list

tiers.name: SSI-Related Medicaid Only
tiers.description: For SSI recipients or those meeting SSI income/asset standards

tiers.name: New Jersey Care/Special Medicaid
tiers.description: For those at or below 100% Federal Poverty Level

tiers.name: NJRAER ABD
tiers.description: For working disabled with slightly higher income

categories.name: Financial Records
items: 5 years of bank statements (if applying for long-term care)
Proof of income from all sources

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records supporting need for long-term care

contentSections.text: If your parent needs long-term care services, any asset transfers in the past 5 years below fair market value can delay eligibility. Keep records of all financial transactions.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for NJ FamilyCare ABD?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify home equity exemptions, but most Medicaid programs allow you to keep your primary residence. Contact your county Board of Social Services for specific rules about home ownership.

faqs.question: What if Dad already has Medicare — will this program still help?
faqs.answer: Yes. If your dad has Medicare, NJ FamilyCare ABD will pay his Medicare premiums and cover costs that Medicare doesn't pay, like copays and deductibles. Medicare remains his primary insurance.

faqs.question: My parent's income is just over the limit — are there any options?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because there are multiple program tiers with different income limits, your parent may qualify for a different tier. The NJRAER ABD program is specifically designed for working disabled individuals with slightly higher incomes.

faqs.question: How long does the 5-year look-back period affect eligibility?
faqs.answer: The 5-year look-back only applies if your parent needs long-term care services. If they transferred assets below fair market value during those 5 years, it could delay eligibility. Basic medical coverage isn't subject to this rule.

faqs.question: Will the state try to recover costs from Mom's estate after she passes away?
faqs.answer: Potentially, but not if there's a surviving spouse, or a child under 21, blind, or disabled. Estate recovery rules apply, but there are important protections for families.

faqs.question: Does my parent's spouse's income count toward the limits?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program considers spousal income and resources when determining eligibility. Both spouses' finances are evaluated together for married couples.

programs.name: Managed Long Term Services and Supports (MLTSS)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or eating, this program covers nursing home care or home-based support.
programs.intro: If your parent needs nursing home-level care but you want them to stay home, New Jersey's MLTSS program can pay for personal care assistants, nursing services, and other support in their own home or assisted living. Your parent must need hands-on help with at least 3 daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, moving from bed to chair, or walking around. Because MLTSS is managed care, one team coordinates all their medical care and support services — no juggling between different providers. The program covers both nursing home care and community-based care, so your parent can receive services at home instead of moving to a facility.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need hands-on assistance with at least 3 activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring from bed to chair, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Age 65+ OR determined disabled by Social Security

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county's Aging and Disability Resource Center if your parent doesn't have Medicaid, or their current Medicaid plan if they do.

steps.title: Determine application path
steps.description: If your parent already has Medicaid, contact their assigned Managed Care Organization. If not, contact your county's Aging and Disability Resource Center.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: A state assessor will evaluate whether your parent meets nursing facility level of care by documenting their need for help with daily activities.

steps.title: Financial eligibility review
steps.description: Provide income, asset, and citizenship documentation. The 60-month look-back period will review any asset transfers.

steps.title: MCO enrollment
steps.description: Once approved, your parent must enroll with a Managed Care Organization to receive services.

applicationGuide.tip: Gather 5 years of financial records before applying — any gifts or below-market sales within 60 months can trigger a penalty period.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits. Each spouse in a couple is evaluated individually at $2,982/month. Limits increase annually in January.

categories.name: Financial
items: Records of any asset transfers or gifts (last 5 years)

categories.name: Medical
items: Social Security disability determination letter (if applicable)

contentSections.text: 60-month look-back rule: Any gifts or below-market sales within 5 years before applying will trigger a penalty period where your parent cannot receive benefits. This includes transfers to family members, even with good intentions.
If your parent's income is over $2,982/month, ask about a Qualified Income Trust (QIT). This legal tool can help them qualify by redirecting excess income, but requires attorney setup.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for MLTSS?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they live there or intend to return, and if the home equity is under $1,097,000 (2025 limit). The primary home doesn't count toward the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is $3,100/month — just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through a Qualified Income Trust (QIT). This legal arrangement redirects the excess income and requires attorney setup, but can make her eligible.

faqs.question: Does having dementia automatically qualify my father?
faqs.answer: No. A dementia diagnosis alone doesn't qualify. He must need hands-on help with at least 3 daily activities like bathing, dressing, or eating. The state will assess his actual functional limitations.

faqs.question: We gave $20,000 to help our daughter buy a house 2 years ago. Will this affect Dad's application?
faqs.answer: Yes. This gift will trigger a penalty period where your father cannot receive MLTSS benefits. The penalty length depends on the gift amount and when it was made within the 60-month look-back period.

faqs.question: Can MLTSS pay for care in assisted living instead of a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes. MLTSS covers long-term care services in your parent's home, assisted living facilities, or nursing homes. The program is designed to help people avoid nursing homes when possible.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular Medicaid?
faqs.answer: MLTSS is Medicaid, but delivered through managed care organizations that coordinate all services. Your parent must meet stricter functional requirements — needing nursing home-level care — but gets more comprehensive long-term support services.

programs.name: Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged and Disabled (PAAD)
programs.tagline: If your parent lives in New Jersey and pays high prescription costs, PAAD can reduce copays to just $5-7 per medication.

localEntities.name: Central processing: P.O. Box 360, Trenton, NJ 08625-0360

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling with prescription costs in New Jersey, PAAD (Pharmaceutical Assistance to the Aged and Disabled) can dramatically reduce what they pay at the pharmacy. Instead of paying full copays through Medicare Part D, your parent will pay just $5 for generic drugs and $7 for brand-name medications. PAAD also helps pay Medicare Part D premiums up to the regional benchmark amount.

This program is for New Jersey residents age 65+ or those receiving Social Security disability benefits. Income limits are generous: $54,943 for single people and $62,390 for married couples filing jointly in 2026. Unlike many programs, PAAD has no asset test — your parent can own their home, have savings, and still qualify.

The key requirement: if your parent has Medicare, they must enroll in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. PAAD works alongside Medicare Part D, not instead of it. Your parent cannot be on Medicaid to qualify for PAAD.
programs.savingsSource: Estimated based on reduced copays from typical Medicare Part D costs to $5-7 per prescription

otherRequirements: Must enroll in Medicare Part D if Medicare-eligible
Do not need to be U.S. citizen

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at NJSaves or call 1-800-792-9745 — processing time varies but most renewals are every 2 years.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of New Jersey residency, age or disability status, income documentation, and Medicare Part D enrollment details.

steps.title: Apply online or by phone
steps.description: Use the NJSaves website (njsave.net) or call 1-800-792-9745. You can also mail a paper application to the Trenton address.

steps.title: Coordinate with Medicare Part D
steps.description: If your parent has Medicare, ensure they're enrolled in a Part D prescription drug plan. PAAD will help pay the premium up to the regional benchmark amount.

steps.title: Start using reduced copays
steps.description: Once approved, your parent pays $5 for generics and $7 for brand-name drugs at New Jersey pharmacies that participate in the state rebate program.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified in sources; most renew every 2 years, some annually
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent's income is just over the PAAD limit, ask about Senior Gold — it's a similar program for higher-income New Jersey residents.

contentSections.heading: 2026 Income Limits
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits; includes Social Security benefits and all other income

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of age (65+) or Social Security Title II Disability benefits

categories.name: Financial
items: Tax returns or Social Security statements showing income
Proof of Medicare Part D enrollment (if Medicare-eligible)

contentSections.text: PAAD only covers prescriptions filled at New Jersey pharmacies from manufacturers that participate in the state rebate program. Out-of-state prescriptions are not covered.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep PAAD if they move to a different state?
faqs.answer: No. PAAD is only for New Jersey residents. If your parent moves out of state, they'll lose PAAD coverage and need to find prescription assistance in their new state.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income goes up and they no longer qualify for PAAD?
faqs.answer: If their income exceeds PAAD limits, ask about Senior Gold — it's another New Jersey program for higher-income residents that provides similar prescription assistance.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to choose between PAAD and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent cannot have both PAAD and Medicaid. If they qualify for Medicaid, they should use Medicaid's prescription benefits instead, which typically provide better coverage.

faqs.question: Will PAAD pay for all of my parent's Medicare Part D premium?
faqs.answer: PAAD pays premiums up to the regional benchmark amount. If your parent chooses a more expensive plan, they'll pay the difference. Plans with no deductible get up to $5 extra coverage.

faqs.question: Can my parent use PAAD at any pharmacy?
faqs.answer: Only at New Jersey pharmacies, and only for medications from manufacturers that participate in the state rebate program. Most major pharmacies and common medications are covered, but always verify before filling prescriptions.

faqs.question: How often does my parent need to reapply for PAAD?
faqs.answer: Most people renew every 2 years, but some must reapply annually. Your parent will receive a renewal notice telling them when to reapply and what documents they need.
